revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.

'''狭穂彦王'''（さほひこおう;生年不詳_垂仁天皇5年（[[紀元前24年]]）10月没）とは、記紀における[[皇族]]（王族）。『[[日本書紀]]』では狭穂彦王、『[[古事記]]』では沙本毘古王。[[彦坐王]]第二王子で、[[開化天皇]]の孫に当たる。[[日下部連]]・[[甲斐国造]]の祖。母は春日建国勝戸売の娘沙本之大闇見戸売（さほのおおくらみとめ）。同母の兄弟に[[葛野別]]・[[近淡海蚊野別]]の祖[[袁邪本王]]、[[若狭耳別]]の祖[[室毘古王]]、[[垂仁天皇]]皇后[[狭穂姫命]]がいる。
垂仁天皇5年に妹の狭穂姫命に天皇暗殺をけしかけるが失敗。叛乱を興すすものの、追い詰められ兄妹ともに稲城の中で自殺する。この「狭穂毘古の叛乱」は、『古事記』』における最も物語性の高い記述とされる。
{{history-stub}} [[Category:古墳時代以前の皇族|おさほのおおきみ]] [[Category:日本神話の人物|おさほのおおきみ]]

revision.comment: 修正
revision.text:
'''狭穂彦王'''（さほひこのみこ、生年不詳 - 垂仁天皇5年10月没）とは、記紀における[[皇族]]（王族）。『[[日本書紀]]』では'''狭穂彦王'''、『[[古事記]]』では'''沙本毘古王'''（さほびこのみこ）。
== 概要 == 『[[記紀]]』には[[垂仁天皇]]5年に妹の[[狭穂姫命]]に天皇暗殺を試みさせるが失敗。叛乱を興すものの、追い詰められ兄妹ともに稲城の中で自殺する。この「[[狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱|狭穂彦王の叛乱]]」は、『[[古事記]]』における最も物語性の高い記述とされる。
== 系譜 == 系譜は『古事記』における記述が最も詳しく、同書によると[[彦坐王]]の子で[[開化天皇]]の孫に当たるとされる。母は春日建国勝戸売の娘である[[沙本之大闇見戸売]]で、同母の兄弟に葛野別・近淡海蚊野別の祖・[[袁邪本王]]、若狭耳別の祖・[[室毘古王]]、[[垂仁天皇]][[皇后]]の[[狭穂姫命]]がいる。また狭穂彦王の孫の阿豆良王は[[日下部氏|日下部連]]の祖で、曾孫（阿豆良王の甥）の[[塩海足尼]]は初代[[甲斐国造]]。
== 関連項目 == *[[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さほひこのみこ}} [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:古墳時代以前に戦死した人物]]

== 関連項目 == *[[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]]

revision.text:
'''狭穂彦王'''（さほひこのみこ、生年不詳 - 垂仁天皇5年（[[BC25年]]）10月没）とは、記紀における[[皇族]]（王族）。『[[日本書紀]]』では'''狭穂彦王'''、『[[古事記]]』では'''沙本毘古王'''（さほびこのみこ）。
== 概要 == 『[[記紀]]』には[[垂仁天皇]]5年に妹の[[狭穂姫命]]に天皇暗殺を試みさせるが失敗。叛乱を興すものの、追い詰められ兄妹ともに稲城の中で自殺する。この「[[狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱|狭穂彦王の叛乱]]」は、『[[古事記]]』における最も物語性の高い記述とされる。
== 系譜 == 系譜は『古事記』における記述が最も詳しく、同書によると[[彦坐王]]の子で[[開化天皇]]の孫に当たるとされる。母は春日建国勝戸売の娘である[[沙本之大闇見戸売]]で、同母の兄弟に葛野別・近淡海蚊野別の祖・[[袁邪本王]]、若狭耳別の祖・[[室毘古王]]、[[垂仁天皇]][[皇后]]の[[狭穂姫命]]がいる。また狭穂彦王の孫の阿豆良王は[[日下部氏|日下部連]]の祖で、曾孫（阿豆良王の甥）の[[塩海足尼]]は初代[[甲斐国造]]。
== 関連項目 == * [[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]] * [[佐久神社（甲府市）]]・・・古墳の上にあった神社を[[天文]]13年（[[1543年]]）8月領主[[向山出雲守]]により移転。
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さほひこのみこ}} [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:古墳時代以前に戦死した人物]]

revision.comment: 戻し：上古人物の西暦併記不適当、地方の俗伝。

revision.comment: Created page with "'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王). == Overview == Accord..."
revision.text:
'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}

[[ja:狭穂彦王]]

'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}

revision.text:
'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}

'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}

'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).

According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.

When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.

According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.

revision.text:
'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}

This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:qq-" to "[[Category:qq deprecated start]]<!--"
revision.text:
'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}

revision.text:
'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}

revision.text:
'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "qq" to "11"
revision.text:
'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}

'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.
== See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "↵↵== See Also ==" to "== See Also =="
revision.text:
'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.== See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}

revision.text:
'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin. == See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧}}

revision.text:
'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|ja|日本書紀|12=draft}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命|12=draft}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱|12=draft}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記|12=draft}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|12=draft}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|ja|開化天皇|12=draft}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王|12=draft}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王|12=draft}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|ja|狭穂姫命|12=draft}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin. == See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=draft}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}}

30 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:狭穂彦王 up to rev 96521766
revision.text:
'''狭穂彦王'''（さほひこのみこ、生年不詳 - 垂仁天皇5年10月）とは、記紀における[[皇族]]（王族）。『[[日本書紀]]』では'''狭穂彦王'''、『[[古事記]]』では'''沙本毘古王'''（さほびこのみこ）。
== 概要 == 『[[記紀]]』には[[垂仁天皇]]5年に妹の[[狭穂姫命]]に天皇暗殺を試みさせるが失敗。叛乱を興すものの、追い詰められ兄妹ともに稲城の中で自殺する。この「[[狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱|狭穂彦王の叛乱]]」は、『[[古事記]]』における最も物語性の高い記述とされる。
== 系譜 == 系譜は『古事記』における記述が最も詳しく、同書によると[[彦坐王]]の子で[[開化天皇]]の孫に当たるとされる。母は春日建国勝戸売の娘である[[沙本之大闇見戸売]]で、同母の兄弟に葛野別・近淡海蚊野別の祖・[[袁邪本王]]、若狭耳別の祖・[[室毘古王]]、[[垂仁天皇]][[皇后]]の[[狭穂姫命]]がいる。
== 関連項目 == *[[飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧]]
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{people-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:さほひこのみこ}} [[Category:古墳時代以前の男性皇族]] [[Category:古墳時代以前に戦死した人物]] [[Category:生没年不詳]]

{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|qid=Q2658252}}|father={{ill|Hikoimasu|qid=Q11487910}}|mother={{ill|Q13360104|qid=Q13360104}}|relatives={{ill|Empress Sahohime|qid=Q11571754}}; {{ill|Q11626514|qid=Q11626514}}; {{ill|Tanba no Michinushi|qid=Q11368587}}; {{ill|Yamashiro-no-Otsutsukimawaka|qid=Q11466268}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11571755]]
Wikidata name: Prince Sahohiko
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. In the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).

== Overview ==

According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, [[:en:Saho-hime|Sahohime no Mikoto]], to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|qq=}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]].

== Genealogy ==

The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikozahowake no Mikoto|qq=}}, making him a grandson of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyamoto no Mikoto|qq=}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murobiko no Mikoto|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135578852}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and [[:en:Saho-hime|Sahohime no Mikoto]], who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.
== See Also ==
* {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of people before the Asuka period|qq=|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}}

He had several siblings, including {{ill|Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyamoto no Mikoto|qq=}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murobiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578852}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and [[:en:Saho-hime|Sahohime no Mikoto]], who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.

'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. In the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).

== Overview ==

According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, [[:en:Saho-hime|Sahohime no Mikoto]], to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|qq=}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]].

== Genealogy ==

The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikozahowake no Mikoto|qq=}}, making him a grandson of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyamoto no Mikoto|qq=}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murobiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578852}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and [[:en:Saho-hime|Sahohime no Mikoto]], who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. In the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).

== Overview ==

According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, [[:en:Saho-hime|Sahohime no Mikoto]], to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|qq=}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]].

== Genealogy ==

The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikozahowake no Mikoto|qq=}}, making him a grandson of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyamoto no Mikoto|qq=}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murobiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578852}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and [[:en:Saho-hime|Sahohime no Mikoto]], who became the empress of Emperor Suinin.
== See Also ==

He had several siblings, including {{ill|Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyamoto no Mikoto|qq=}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murobiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578852}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and [[:en:Saho-hime|Sahohime no Mikoto]], who became the empress of Emperor Suinin. == See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of people before the Asuka period|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:狭穂彦王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:狭穂彦王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:狭穂彦王]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|father={{ill|Hikoimasu|WD=Q11487910}}|mother={{ill|Q13360104|WD=Q13360104}}|relatives={{ill|Empress Sahohime|WD=Q11571754}}; {{ill|Q11626514|WD=Q11626514}}; {{ill|Tanba no Michinushi|WD=Q11368587}}; {{ill|Yamashiro-no-Otsutsukimawaka|WD=Q11466268}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11571755]] Wikidata name: Prince Sahohiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:00, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. In the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, [[:en:Saho-hime|Sahohime no Mikoto]], to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|qq=}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]].
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]]. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikozahowake no Mikoto|qq=}}, making him a grandson of [[:en:Emperor Kaika|Emperor Kaika]]. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyamoto no Mikoto|qq=}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murobiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578852}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and [[:en:Saho-hime|Sahohime no Mikoto]], who became the empress of Emperor Suinin. == See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of people before the Asuka period|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:狭穂彦王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:狭穂彦王]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:狭穂彦王]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097}}|nationality={{ill|Yamato Kinship|WD=Q2658252}}|father={{ill|Hikoimasu|WD=Q11487910}}|mother={{ill|Q13360104|WD=Q13360104}}|relatives={{ill|Empress Sahohime|WD=Q11571754}}; {{ill|Q11626514|WD=Q11626514}}; {{ill|Tanba no Michinushi|WD=Q11368587}}; {{ill|Yamashiro-no-Otsutsukimawaka|WD=Q11466268}}}} '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''' (狭穂彦王, dates of birth and death unknown, died in the 10th month of the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign) was a member of the imperial family (royalty) in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, he is referred to as '''Sahohiko no Mikoto''', while in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, he is called '''Sahobiko no Mikoto''' (沙本毘古王).
== Overview ==
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, in the 5th year of Emperor Suinin's reign, Sahohiko ordered his sister, {{ill|Saho-hime|en|Saho-hime|WD=Q11571754|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto}}, to assassinate the emperor. When the plan failed, he initiated a rebellion but was ultimately cornered. Both Sahohiko and his sister committed suicide within the fortifications of an earthen fortress made of rice sheaves. This rebellion, known as the {{ill|Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|ja|狭穂姫命#狭穂彦王の叛乱|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rebellion of Sahohime and Sahohiko|qq=}}, is considered one of the most vividly described events in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}.
== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikozahowake no Mikoto|qq=}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyamoto no Mikoto|qq=}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murobiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578852}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|en|Saho-hime|WD=Q11571754|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin. == See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of people before the Asuka period|1=List of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}}

== Genealogy ==
The genealogy of Sahohiko is most detailed in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}. According to it, he was the son of {{ill|Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|lt=Hikozahowake no Mikoto|ja|彦坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikozahowake no Mikoto|qq=}}, making him a grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kaika|en|Emperor Kaika|WD=Q333548|lt=Emperor Kaika|1=Emperor Kaika}}. His mother was Sahono Ōkurumitoori (沙本之大闇見戸売), the daughter of Kasuga Takakuni Katsutorihime (春日建国勝戸売). He had several siblings, including {{ill|Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Ozaho no Ōkimi|lt=Oyamoto no Mikoto|ja|袁邪本王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Oyamoto no Mikoto|qq=}}, the ancestor of Kudonowake and Kotoomi Kano, {{ill|Murobiko no Mikoto|ja|室毘古王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murobiko no Mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|WD=Q135578852|1=Murobiko no Mikoto}}, the ancestor of Wakasa Mimibetsu, and {{ill|Saho-hime|en|Saho-hime|WD=Q11571754|lt=Sahohime no Mikoto|1=Saho-hime}}, who became the empress of Emperor Suinin. == See Also == * {{ill|List of people before the Asuka period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of people before the Asuka period|1=list of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11665973}}
